content.text: Azure AI Foundry Agent Service (formerly Azure OpenAI Assistants) is Microsoft's managed agent runtime built on top of Azure OpenAI. You create an
with a model deployment, instructions, and optional built-in tools (Code Interpreter, File Search) or custom function tools. The service manages
(the reasoning loop). Everything is hosted on Azure — no inference infrastructure to manage.

attrs.alt: Azure AI Foundry Agent Service architecture

content.text: a configured model instance — model deployment, instructions, and attached tools. Persists in Azure; reusable across many conversations.
a conversation context stored in Azure. Every user message and assistant reply is appended as a
. The full thread history is sent to the model on each run.
one execution of the agent's reasoning loop on a thread. Transitions through
when a function tool needs calling).
built-in tool that generates and executes Python in a sandboxed Azure container — useful for data analysis and chart generation.
built-in RAG tool backed by Azure AI Search. Upload documents; the agent retrieves relevant chunks automatically.
custom functions your code exposes to the agent via JSON Schema definitions.
— it tries, in order: environment variables, Managed Identity, Azure CLI, VS Code credentials. For local development, run

from azure.ai.projects import AIProjectClient
from azure.ai.projects.models import FileSearchTool, CodeInterpreterTool
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential

# Connect to your Azure AI Foundry project
client = AIProjectClient.from_connection_string(
    conn_str="eastus.api.azureml.ms;00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000;my-rg;my-project",
    credential=DefaultAzureCredential(),
)

# Create an agent
agent = client.agents.create_agent(
    model="gpt-4o",           # must be deployed in your AI Foundry project
    name="data-analyst",
    instructions="""You are a data analyst assistant.
When given a dataset, analyze it thoroughly and produce clear visualisations.
Always explain your findings in plain language before presenting charts.""",
    tools=[
        CodeInterpreterTool(),    # executes Python in a sandboxed environment
    ],
    headers={"x-ms-enable-preview": "true"},
)
print("Agent ID:", agent.id)
) must match a deployment in your Azure AI Foundry project, not the canonical OpenAI model name.
Creating a Thread and Sending Messages

# A Thread holds the conversation history.
# All messages (user and assistant) are stored in Azure and
# automatically included in every subsequent model call.

thread = client.agents.create_thread()
print("Thread ID:", thread.id)

# Send the first user message
client.agents.create_message(
    thread_id=thread.id,
    role="user",
    content="Attached is a CSV of monthly sales. Which product line has the fastest growth?",
    # Attach a file uploaded earlier (see File Upload section)
    attachments=[{"file_id": "file-abc123", "tools": [{"type": "code_interpreter"}]}],
)
Threads are long-lived — a thread can contain hundreds of messages. Bedrock Agents uses session IDs with a TTL; Azure AI threads are permanent until deleted.
A Run triggers the agent's reasoning loop. You either poll its status or use the streaming API:

import time
from azure.ai.projects.models import RunStatus

# Create a Run — this triggers the agent's reasoning loop
run = client.agents.create_run(thread_id=thread.id, agent_id=agent.id)

# Poll until the run reaches a terminal state
while run.status in (RunStatus.QUEUED, RunStatus.IN_PROGRESS, RunStatus.REQUIRES_ACTION):
    time.sleep(1)
    run = client.agents.get_run(thread_id=thread.id, run_id=run.id)

    if run.status == RunStatus.REQUIRES_ACTION:
        # Handle function tool calls (see Function Tools section)
        tool_outputs = execute_function_calls(run.required_action.submit_tool_outputs.tool_calls)
        run = client.agents.submit_tool_outputs_to_run(
            thread_id=thread.id, run_id=run.id, tool_outputs=tool_outputs
        )

if run.status == RunStatus.COMPLETED:
    messages = client.agents.list_messages(thread_id=thread.id)
    # Messages are in reverse-chronological order; [0] is the latest assistant reply
    latest = messages.data[0]
    print(latest.content[0].text.value)
else:
    print("Run failed:", run.last_error)
, the model has decided to call one or more of your function tools. Submit the tool outputs to resume the run.
for a real-time streaming experience. The stream context manager handles the run lifecycle and routes
Define Python functions with type hints and docstrings; the SDK derives the JSON Schema automatically via
. The agent calls these when it determines the user's intent matches the function's description.
the model fills in arguments; validate them in your function before calling external services
return a JSON string describing the error — the agent can relay it to the user or retry with different arguments
and the async client variant if your function tools are async
Upload documents to Azure and create a vector store. The agent automatically retrieves relevant chunks before generating responses — no retrieval code needed.

from azure.ai.projects.models import FileSearchTool, VectorStoreDataSource, VectorStoreDataSourceAssetType

# Upload documents
with open("policy.pdf", "rb") as f:
    uploaded_file = client.agents.upload_file_and_poll(f, purpose="assistants")

# Create a vector store from the uploaded file
vector_store = client.agents.create_vector_store_and_poll(
    file_ids=[uploaded_file.id],
    name="policy-docs",
)

# Create an agent with File Search enabled
agent = client.agents.create_agent(
    model="gpt-4o",
    name="policy-assistant",
    instructions="Answer questions about company policies using only the provided documents.",
    tools=[FileSearchTool()],
    tool_resources={"file_search": {"vector_store_ids": [vector_store.id]}},
)

# From here the agent automatically:
# 1. Embeds the user query
# 2. Retrieves relevant chunks from the vector store
# 3. Injects retrieved chunks into the FM prompt
# 4. Generates a grounded response with citations
Supported formats: PDF, DOCX, TXT, HTML, CSV, JSON, Markdown, and more
The vector store is backed by Azure AI Search Serverless; embeddings use Azure OpenAI text-embedding-3-large by default
Citations appear in assistant message content as
objects, giving you the source file and chunk text
Code Interpreter lets the agent generate and run Python code in a sandboxed Azure environment. It can process uploaded files (CSV, Excel, images), perform numerical analysis, and produce PNG charts.

from azure.ai.projects.models import CodeInterpreterTool

# Code Interpreter runs Python in a sandboxed Azure container.
# The agent can: generate and execute code, create charts, parse files.
agent = client.agents.create_agent(
    model="gpt-4o",
    name="data-scientist",
    instructions="Analyse data and produce visualisations. Save charts as PNG files.",
    tools=[CodeInterpreterTool()],
)

# After a run completes, retrieve any generated files (e.g. charts):
messages = client.agents.list_messages(thread_id=thread.id)
for msg in messages.data:
    for block in msg.content:
        if hasattr(block, "image_file"):
            file_id = block.image_file.file_id
            file_content = client.agents.get_file_content(file_id)
            with open(f"chart_{file_id}.png", "wb") as out:
                out.write(file_content)
            print(f"Saved chart: chart_{file_id}.png")
The sandbox has NumPy, pandas, matplotlib, scikit-learn, and other data-science packages pre-installed
Generated files (charts, processed CSVs) are stored in Azure and retrievable via
Each run gets an isolated sandbox; no state persists between runs
Comparing Azure AI Foundry vs Amazon Bedrock Agents
Azure AI resources persist and accumulate storage costs until deleted. Clean up after testing:

# Always delete agents, threads, and vector stores when done —
# they persist and incur storage costs until explicitly deleted.

client.agents.delete_vector_store(vector_store.id)
client.agents.delete_file(uploaded_file.id)
client.agents.delete_agent(agent.id)
client.agents.delete_thread(thread.id)  # deletes all messages in the thread
the agent creation succeeds but runs fail with
if the model name doesn't match an actual deployment in your project — check the Deployments tab in AI Foundry portal
if you don't submit tool outputs within the run's timeout, the run moves to
Azure sends the full thread history on each run; very long threads hit the model's context limit — implement periodic thread summarisation or start a new thread
uploaded files and vector stores persist until deleted and are counted against your storage quota — delete files when the agent no longer needs them
the Agent Service shares rate limits with your Azure OpenAI deployment; at high concurrency, use separate deployments for agents vs. completion endpoints
explicitly — the credential chain falls back silently and may pick the wrong identity

Agents can delegate sub-tasks to other agents by calling them as function tools. One agent acts as the orchestrator; specialist agents handle specific domains (data analysis, translation, code review). The orchestrator thread accumulates results from each specialist.

# Azure AI Agent Service supports agent-to-agent calls (preview).
# One agent can act as an orchestrator that delegates sub-tasks to specialist agents.

from azure.ai.projects import AIProjectClient
from azure.ai.projects.models import FunctionTool
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
import json

client = AIProjectClient.from_connection_string(
    conn_str="eastus.api.azureml.ms;00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000;my-rg;my-proj",
    credential=DefaultAzureCredential(),
)

# Specialist: data analyst
analyst_agent = client.agents.create_agent(
    model="gpt-4o",
    name="data-analyst",
    instructions="Analyse numeric data and return JSON summaries. Be concise.",
    tools=[],
)

# Orchestrator function that calls the analyst agent
def delegate_to_analyst(data_description: str, csv_data: str) -> str:
    """Ask the data analyst agent to summarise the given CSV data."""
    thread = client.agents.create_thread()
    client.agents.create_message(thread_id=thread.id, role="user",
        content=f"{data_description}\n\n{csv_data}")
    run = client.agents.create_and_process_run(thread_id=thread.id, agent_id=analyst_agent.id)
    msgs = client.agents.list_messages(thread_id=thread.id)
    return msgs.data[0].content[0].text.value

# Orchestrator agent knows about the analyst tool
orchestrator = client.agents.create_agent(
    model="gpt-4o",
    name="orchestrator",
    instructions="""You coordinate analysis tasks.
When the user gives you data, delegate the analysis to the analyst tool,
then present the findings in a clear executive summary.""",
    tools=FunctionTool(functions=[delegate_to_analyst]).definitions,
)
each specialist agent has its own thread — errors in one don't corrupt the orchestrator's history
each agent can use different models (e.g., GPT-4o for the orchestrator, o3 for reasoning tasks) and different tool sets
each agent-to-agent call adds a full round-trip; batch multiple sub-tasks if possible
Run steps expose the agent's internal actions — tool calls, their arguments, and outputs. Use this for debugging, audit logging, and billing attribution.
steps show function calls and their outputs
steps show when the agent produced a text reply
Code Interpreter steps include the generated code and the output (stdout, generated files)
File Search steps show which vector store chunks were retrieved
For production deployments, threads need active management to prevent context overflow and control storage costs:

import time
from datetime import datetime, timedelta

# ── Thread summarisation: prevent context overflow ──
# When a thread gets long, summarise it and start a new thread
# seeded with the summary.

def get_thread_message_count(thread_id: str) -> int:
    messages = client.agents.list_messages(thread_id=thread_id)
    return len(messages.data)

def summarise_and_restart(old_thread_id: str, agent_id: str) -> str:
    """Summarise the old thread and start a fresh one."""
    # Ask the agent for a summary in the existing thread
    client.agents.create_message(thread_id=old_thread_id, role="user",
        content="Please summarise our conversation so far in 200 words or fewer.")
    run = client.agents.create_and_process_run(thread_id=old_thread_id, agent_id=agent_id)
    msgs = client.agents.list_messages(thread_id=old_thread_id)
    summary = msgs.data[0].content[0].text.value

    # Seed the new thread with the summary as system context
    new_thread = client.agents.create_thread()
    client.agents.create_message(thread_id=new_thread.id, role="user",
        content=f"[Context from previous conversation]\n{summary}\n\n"
                "Please continue helping me from here.")
    return new_thread.id

# Usage: summarise after every 40 messages
if get_thread_message_count(thread.id) > 40:
    thread_id = summarise_and_restart(thread.id, agent.id)
GPT-4o has a 128K-token context window; a thread with many long messages will eventually overflow — summarise and restart
long threads cost more per run because all prior messages are included in every model call — summarisation reduces ongoing token costs
store thread IDs in your own database keyed by user ID so you can resume conversations across sessions
each thread supports only one active run at a time — if you need concurrent runs for the same user, use separate threads
is Microsoft's managed agent runtime using threads, runs, and optional built-in tools (Code Interpreter, File Search)
store the full conversation history in Azure; the same thread ID resumes the conversation across sessions
execute the reasoning loop — poll status or use streaming; handle
executes sandboxed Python for data analysis; File Search provides managed RAG over uploaded documents
define custom actions via JSON Schema; the agent extracts parameters and your code executes the action
agents, threads, and vector stores — they persist until deleted and incur storage costs
